title: Lawfulness, Fairness and Transparency
description: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly, and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject. The organisation must be able to identify a valid lawful basis for each processing activity and communicate processing activities clearly to individuals.
guidance: Article 5(1)(a) UK GDPR establishes that processing must have a lawful basis under Article 6 (and Article 9 for special category data), must not be deceptive or harmful to individuals, and must provide sufficient information to data subjects so they understand how their data is used. Transparency is operationalised through privacy notices, layered disclosures, and internal processing records.
implementationGuide: Begin by mapping every processing activity in your organisation to a specific lawful basis using a tool such as OneTrust Data Mapping or BigID to catalogue data flows automatically. Ensure privacy notices are written in plain language and are accessible at every collection point, avoiding vague or catch-all language that regulators consider opaque. Configure your consent and preference management platform (e.g., Didomi or Cookiebot) to surface the correct notice version based on jurisdiction and channel. The 'done' state is achieved when every processing activity in the ROPA has a documented lawful basis and a corresponding privacy notice that has been reviewed by legal counsel within the past 12 months. A common failure mode is copying a generic privacy notice template without tailoring it to your actual processing activities, which creates a mismatch between what is disclosed and what occurs in practice. Review notices whenever a new processing activity is introduced or a material change to existing processing occurs.
exampleEvidence: Privacy notice version-controlled document with legal sign-off date and change log
ROPA extract showing lawful basis column populated for each processing activity
OneTrust consent receipt logs demonstrating notice was served at point of collection
Legal review memo confirming notice language meets ICO transparency guidance
Staff procedure document describing the process for updating notices on processing changes
Screenshot of live website/app showing layered privacy notice at data collection point

title: Purpose Limitation
description: Personal data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes. Any new use of personal data must be assessed for compatibility with the original collection purpose.
guidance: Article 5(1)(b) requires organisations to define purposes before collection and not repurpose data without a legitimate justification or fresh consent. The compatibility test considers the link between purposes, context of collection, nature and sensitivity of data, possible consequences, and safeguards in place. Further processing for archiving, research, or statistical purposes may be permissible under specific conditions.
implementationGuide: Document each processing purpose explicitly in your ROPA using OneTrust or Informatica Axon Data Governance so that downstream teams cannot invent new uses without a formal review. Establish a 'new use' intake process that routes requests through a purpose compatibility assessment, with sign-off from the DPO before any repurposing commences. Integrate purpose tags into your data catalogue so that analytical and engineering teams can see permitted uses at the point of access. The 'done' state is when no data set in production is being used for a purpose not recorded in the ROPA, verified through periodic data usage audits. A common failure mode is analytics teams deriving secondary insights from operational data without triggering the compatibility review, effectively creating undocumented processing activities. Automated data lineage tools can detect new query patterns and alert the privacy team when data is accessed outside its tagged purposes.
exampleEvidence: ROPA entries with explicit purpose statements for each processing activity
Purpose compatibility assessment template and a completed example assessment
New processing intake form requiring purpose description and DPO sign-off
Data catalogue screenshot showing purpose tags applied to key datasets
DPO decision log recording approved and rejected repurposing requests
Audit report confirming no undocumented processing activities were identified in scope

title: Data Minimisation
description: Personal data collected and processed must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed. Organisations must avoid collecting more data than is needed and must periodically review whether held data remains necessary.
guidance: Article 5(1)(c) targets the over-collection and prolonged retention of personal data. Minimisation applies both at collection (forms, APIs, integrations) and over time (deletion schedules, anonymisation). Privacy by design practices should embed minimisation into system development, preventing unnecessary fields from being captured in the first place.
implementationGuide: Conduct a data minimisation review for all active data collection forms and API endpoints, using BigID or Microsoft Purview to discover personal data fields that have not been accessed or used within a defined period. Work with engineering teams to remove or pseudonymise fields that serve no active business purpose, documenting the rationale in the ROPA. Implement field-level controls in your CRM, marketing platform, and HR system to prevent the capture of unnecessary attributes. The 'done' state is achieved when every data field in key systems has a documented business justification and a designated owner accountable for its retention. A common failure mode is legacy integrations that were built to capture 'everything just in case', resulting in large volumes of data with no clear purpose; these must be identified and remediated through automated discovery scans.
exampleEvidence: Data minimisation audit report listing reviewed fields and remediation actions taken
Before-and-after screenshots of a registration form following field reduction exercise
BigID or Microsoft Purview scan results showing data fields discovered and assessed
Engineering ticket demonstrating removal or pseudonymisation of unnecessary data fields
ROPA column confirming data categories collected are marked as 'adequate and necessary'
Privacy by design checklist completed for a new product feature requiring data collection

title: Accuracy
description: Personal data must be accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date. Every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that is inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which it is processed, is erased or rectified without delay.
guidance: Article 5(1)(d) places an ongoing obligation to maintain data quality. This includes implementing processes for individuals to update their own data, periodic verification of records, and downstream updates when corrections are made. Inaccurate data can cause harm to individuals (e.g., incorrect health records, erroneous credit assessments) and expose the organisation to regulatory risk.
implementationGuide: Implement self-service data correction portals or preference centres through tools such as OneTrust or DataGrail so data subjects can update their records directly without requiring manual intervention. Establish automated data quality checks in your data pipelines using Microsoft Purview Data Quality or Informatica Data Quality to flag anomalies such as malformed addresses, duplicate records, or stale entries. Define a data quality SLA that specifies the maximum age of key data categories before mandatory re-verification. The 'done' state is when accuracy checks are embedded in at least all high-risk processing activities (HR, health, financial) and a self-service correction mechanism is live. A common failure mode is running accuracy checks on ingestion but not applying them to data already at rest, leaving historical records unchecked and potentially inaccurate for years.
exampleEvidence: Self-service account/preference portal screenshot demonstrating data correction functionality
Data quality dashboard showing percentage of records passing accuracy checks by system
Rectification log recording data subject correction requests and completion dates
Data quality policy defining accuracy standards, SLAs, and responsible teams
Automated data quality rule configuration in Informatica or Microsoft Purview
Evidence of periodic re-verification campaign (e.g., email asking customers to confirm details)

title: Storage Limitation
description: Personal data must be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data is processed. Organisations must establish and enforce retention schedules for all categories of personal data.
guidance: Article 5(1)(e) requires organisations to define and apply maximum retention periods. Data must be securely deleted or anonymised once the retention period expires. Exceptions exist for archiving in the public interest, scientific or historical research, and statistical purposes, subject to appropriate safeguards. Retention schedules must account for both legal minimum retention obligations and maximum privacy-driven limits.
implementationGuide: Develop a comprehensive retention schedule covering all personal data categories, mapped to business process, legal obligation, and risk justification, and maintain it in a governance tool such as OneTrust or TrustArc. Automate retention enforcement using Microsoft Purview Retention Policies or equivalent platform controls so that deletion or anonymisation triggers without manual intervention at the end of each retention period. Conduct an annual retention schedule review to capture new data categories and changes in applicable law. The 'done' state is when automated deletion jobs are running and a scheduled report confirms that no data category has exceeded its defined retention limit across primary systems. A common failure mode is defining a retention schedule but failing to implement automated enforcement, leaving data in systems indefinitely because deletion requires manual effort that never gets prioritised.
exampleEvidence: Approved retention schedule document covering all personal data categories with review date
Automated deletion job logs showing records removed in the past quarter per schedule
Microsoft Purview retention policy configuration screenshot for key data stores
Annual retention schedule review minutes or sign-off record
Data inventory extract confirming no data category exceeds its scheduled retention period
Anonymisation procedure document and a sample output demonstrating re-identification is not possible

title: Integrity and Confidentiality
description: Personal data must be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction, or damage, using appropriate technical and organisational measures.
guidance: Article 5(1)(f) establishes the security principle as one of the core data protection principles. It requires a risk-based approach to security that considers the nature of the data, the likelihood and severity of threats, and the cost and effectiveness of available safeguards. This principle is operationalised through Article 32 (security of processing) and links directly to breach notification obligations under Articles 33 and 34.
implementationGuide: Maintain an information security programme aligned to ISO 27001 or NIST CSF that explicitly addresses personal data risks, and document the technical and organisational measures (TOMs) applied to each processing activity in your ROPA. Deploy data loss prevention controls using Microsoft Purview Information Protection or Symantec DLP to detect and block unauthorised exfiltration of personal data. Implement encryption at rest and in transit for all personal data stores, and manage encryption key lifecycle through a dedicated key management service. The 'done' state is when TOMs are documented for every high-risk processing activity, encryption is confirmed through automated infrastructure scans, and DLP policies are active across email, endpoints, and cloud storage. A common failure mode is documenting TOMs at project inception but failing to verify they remain in place as infrastructure evolves, particularly after cloud migrations or third-party integrations.
exampleEvidence: Technical and organisational measures (TOMs) register linked to ROPA processing activities
Encryption-at-rest and in-transit policy with evidence of implementation across key systems
Microsoft Purview or Symantec DLP policy configuration and recent incident/alert report
Penetration test report or vulnerability scan confirming security controls are effective
Access control review showing principle of least privilege applied to personal data systems
ISO 27001 certificate or equivalent security assurance report covering personal data assets

title: Identifying and Documenting Lawful Basis
description: Every processing activity must be grounded in one of the six lawful bases defined in Article 6 UK GDPR. The chosen basis must be identified before processing begins and documented in the Record of Processing Activities, with the rationale for the selection recorded.
guidance: Article 6 provides six bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, and legitimate interests. Selecting the wrong basis is not a minor administrative error — it can invalidate an entire processing activity and trigger enforcement action. The basis must be appropriate for the processing in question; organisations cannot switch bases post-hoc when challenged.
implementationGuide: Build a lawful basis decision matrix that guides process owners through a structured selection process, and embed this into your privacy programme using OneTrust or TrustArc as the system of record for documented decisions. Require every new processing activity proposed by business teams to complete a lawful basis justification form before data collection begins, with DPO or legal review for novel activities. Integrate the lawful basis field into your ROPA template so it is mandatory and cannot be left blank. The 'done' state is when every ROPA entry has a populated, reviewed lawful basis field and the rationale behind the selection is recorded alongside it. A common failure mode is defaulting to consent as the lawful basis for all processing because it feels 'safe', when in fact contract or legal obligation is more appropriate and consent withdrawal would then create operational disruption.
exampleEvidence: ROPA extract showing lawful basis and documented rationale for each processing activity
Lawful basis decision matrix or flowchart used by teams to select appropriate basis
New processing intake form requiring lawful basis justification with DPO sign-off
DPO guidance note on selecting between contract and legitimate interests for key use cases
Internal audit finding confirming all in-scope processing activities have documented bases
Training material slide deck covering the six lawful bases with worked examples

title: Consent Mechanisms
description: Where consent is the chosen lawful basis, it must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Consent must be obtained through a clear affirmative action, must be as easy to withdraw as to give, and records of consent must be maintained.
guidance: Article 7 sets specific conditions for consent. Pre-ticked boxes, bundled consent, and consent as a condition of service are not valid. Organisations must keep records proving when, how, and what an individual consented to. Withdrawal of consent must be honoured without detriment, and the organisation must be able to demonstrate compliance with these requirements.
implementationGuide: Deploy a consent and preference management platform such as Didomi, Cookiebot, or OneTrust Consent Management to capture, store, and honour consent signals across all digital channels. Configure the platform to record a timestamped consent receipt for each individual, capturing the version of the notice presented, the specific purposes consented to, and the collection channel. Build withdrawal flows that propagate consent revocation downstream to all processing systems within a defined SLA (recommended: 72 hours for marketing suppression). The 'done' state is when every consent-based processing activity is served by the CMP, consent receipts are queryable for individual data subjects, and withdrawal propagation is automated and tested. A common failure mode is deploying a CMP on the website but failing to connect it to downstream CRM and email systems, so withdrawals are not honoured in practice even when recorded.
exampleEvidence: Didomi or Cookiebot CMP configuration screenshot showing consent purposes and UI design
Sample consent receipt log showing timestamp, notice version, and purposes consented to
Withdrawal propagation procedure document with defined SLA and system integration map
Test evidence demonstrating consent withdrawal successfully suppresses downstream processing
Consent rate reporting dashboard showing opt-in rates by channel and purpose
Legal review of consent wording confirming compliance with Article 7 requirements

title: Special Category Data Processing
description: Processing of special category data — including health, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, genetic data, biometric data, sex life or sexual orientation — is prohibited unless a specific condition in Article 9(2) is met in addition to a lawful basis under Article 6.
guidance: Article 9 requires organisations to identify both an Article 6 lawful basis and an Article 9(2) condition for any special category processing. In the UK, Schedule 1 of the Data Protection Act 2018 provides additional conditions and substantial public interest bases. The processing must be documented with greater rigour, and a DPA Schedule 1 condition (where applicable) must be supported by an appropriate policy document.
implementationGuide: Maintain a special category data register as a subset of your ROPA, using OneTrust or Informatica Axon to tag and track all processing activities involving Article 9 data categories. For each entry, document the Article 9(2) condition relied upon, the corresponding DPA 2018 Schedule 1 condition where applicable, and whether an appropriate policy document is in place. Conduct a targeted DPIA for all special category processing activities regardless of whether one would otherwise be required. The 'done' state is when every special category processing activity is identified, has a documented dual legal basis, and where required under DPA 2018 has an appropriate policy document signed off by the DPO. A common failure mode is collecting sensitive data through generic intake forms (e.g., equality monitoring) without triggering the special category review process because the data owner does not recognise it as falling within Article 9.
exampleEvidence: Special category data register with Article 9(2) conditions and DPA 2018 Schedule 1 references
Appropriate policy document(s) required under DPA 2018 Schedule 1 for in-scope processing
DPIA conducted for a special category processing activity with DPO sign-off
Data discovery scan results (BigID or similar) confirming special category data locations
Process owner attestation confirming awareness of special category obligations
Legal review memo confirming dual legal basis for each special category processing activity

title: Criminal Conviction Data
description: Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences may only be carried out under the control of official authority or when authorised by UK law, as required by Article 10 UK GDPR and further specified in the Data Protection Act 2018.
guidance: Article 10 UK GDPR, read with Schedule 1 Part 3 DPA 2018, restricts criminal conviction data processing to specific circumstances. Most private organisations will only be able to process this data in the context of safeguarding, legal claims, or background screening under DBS regulations. An appropriate policy document is required under Schedule 1 where the Schedule 1 condition is relied upon.
implementationGuide: Identify all business processes that involve criminal conviction data — most commonly recruitment screening, safeguarding, insurance underwriting, and fraud prevention — and register them in your ROPA using Microsoft Purview or OneTrust with a specific 'Article 10' flag. For each activity, confirm that a DPA 2018 Schedule 1 Part 3 condition applies and that an appropriate policy document exists and is reviewed annually. Ensure that DBS check processes are conducted through an umbrella body or direct channel authorised by the DBS and that results are not retained beyond the permitted period. The 'done' state is when all criminal conviction data processing activities are identified, have a valid Schedule 1 condition, and are covered by an appropriate policy document. A common failure mode is HR teams retaining copies of DBS certificates beyond the recommended period of six months after the recruitment decision, creating unnecessary risk.
exampleEvidence: ROPA entries for criminal conviction data processing with Article 10 basis documented
Appropriate policy document for DPA 2018 Schedule 1 Part 3 conditions relied upon
DBS check procedure document including retention limit and destruction confirmation process
Evidence that DBS check results are not retained beyond permitted period (e.g., destruction log)
Legal review confirming Schedule 1 conditions are met for each identified processing activity
Staff guidance on handling criminal conviction data during recruitment and safeguarding processes

title: Legitimate Interests Assessment
description: Where legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f)) is relied upon as the lawful basis, a Legitimate Interests Assessment must be conducted to confirm that the interests are genuine, that processing is necessary and proportionate, and that individual rights do not override the controller's interests.
guidance: The LIA involves a three-part test: purpose test (is the interest legitimate?), necessity test (is processing necessary for that purpose?), and balancing test (do individual rights override the legitimate interest?). The assessment must be documented and reviewed when circumstances change. Legitimate interests cannot be used for processing that overrides fundamental rights or where individuals would not reasonably expect the processing.
implementationGuide: Develop a standardised LIA template aligned to ICO guidance and store completed assessments in your privacy management platform such as OneTrust or TrustArc, linked to the corresponding ROPA entry. Require all process owners relying on legitimate interests to complete an LIA before processing begins, with DPO review for any assessment that reaches the balancing test stage. Schedule annual reviews of standing LIAs to ensure they remain valid as processing or context changes. The 'done' state is when every ROPA entry citing legitimate interests has a completed, reviewed, and approved LIA document on file. A common failure mode is treating the LIA as a one-time checkbox exercise rather than a living document, so assessments become stale and no longer reflect actual processing activities or the current balance of interests.
exampleEvidence: Completed LIA documents for each processing activity relying on legitimate interests basis
LIA template document showing the three-part test structure (purpose, necessity, balancing)
DPO approval record for LIAs that passed through formal review
Annual LIA review schedule and evidence of completed reviews
ROPA extract linking legitimate interests entries to corresponding LIA reference numbers
Example of an LIA that concluded processing was not justified, with alternative approach taken

title: Right to be Informed — Privacy Notices
description: Data subjects must be provided with clear and transparent information about the processing of their personal data at the time of collection (Article 13) or within one month if data is obtained from a third party (Article 14). Information must include identity of controller, purposes, lawful basis, retention periods, and data subject rights.
guidance: Articles 13 and 14 specify the information that must be provided. Privacy notices must be concise, transparent, intelligible, and in an easily accessible form, using clear and plain language. Layered notices are recommended by the ICO. The information provided must match actual processing, and notices must be kept current as processing activities change.
implementationGuide: Maintain a master privacy notice inventory mapping each notice to the processing activities it covers, managed within OneTrust or TrustArc, and set automated review reminders when processing changes are logged in the ROPA. Implement a layered notice approach with a short-form notice at the point of collection and a full notice linked from every touchpoint. Use plain language testing (Flesch-Kincaid or equivalent) to verify readability before publishing. The 'done' state is when every data collection point has an associated, current privacy notice, all required Article 13/14 information is present, and notices have been reviewed by legal within the past 12 months. A common failure mode is publishing a compliant notice but failing to link to it from all collection points — for example, a new mobile app or third-party integration that launches without a connected notice.
exampleEvidence: Privacy notice inventory mapping each notice to collection points and processing activities
Live privacy notice with all required Article 13 fields present and verified by legal
Readability test results (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid score) for published privacy notice
Annual privacy notice review record with legal sign-off and change log
Website/app screenshots confirming notice links are present at all data collection touchpoints
Third-party collection notice (Article 14) template used when data is sourced indirectly

title: Right of Access — Subject Access Requests
description: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation of whether their personal data is being processed and, if so, to receive a copy of that data along with supplementary information. The organisation must respond to SARs within one calendar month (extendable by two further months for complex or numerous requests).
guidance: Article 15 establishes the right of access. Responses must include the personal data itself, the purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, and details of data subject rights. Third-party data within records must be redacted. The one-month deadline runs from receipt of a valid request and cannot be extended without informing the requester.
implementationGuide: Implement a centralised SAR intake and workflow management system such as DataGrail or OneTrust DSAR module to receive, triage, assign, and track all subject access requests against the statutory deadline. Integrate the platform with key data stores (CRM, HR system, email archives) to automate data discovery and reduce manual effort in compiling responses. Establish a standard response template covering all Article 15 supplementary information requirements. The 'done' state is when all SARs are logged in a single system, average response time is within 30 days, and no requests have exceeded the statutory deadline in the past 12 months. A common failure mode is relying on email-based SAR handling without a tracking system, causing requests to be missed or responded to late, particularly during periods of high volume or staff absence.
exampleEvidence: SAR log showing all requests received, assigned, and completed within the past 12 months
DataGrail or OneTrust DSAR workflow configuration screenshot
Standard SAR response template including all Article 15 supplementary information fields
Average response time report confirming responses are within the 30-day statutory period
Third-party redaction procedure document and a sample redacted response
SAR handling policy and staff procedure guide including escalation and extension process

title: Right to Rectification
description: Data subjects have the right to have inaccurate personal data corrected without undue delay. They also have the right to have incomplete personal data completed, including by means of providing a supplementary statement. The organisation must act on valid rectification requests promptly.
guidance: Article 16 requires organisations to correct inaccurate data and to consider requests to complete incomplete data. A response must be provided within one calendar month. Where personal data has been disclosed to third parties, those parties must be informed of the rectification unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort.
implementationGuide: Establish a rectification request intake process, ideally through a self-service portal (DataGrail or OneTrust) that routes requests to data owners in the relevant system. For each request, verify the individual's identity, confirm the inaccuracy, update the record in the source system, and propagate corrections to all downstream systems and processors that hold a copy of the data. Maintain a rectification log recording all requests, actions taken, and third parties notified. The 'done' state is when rectification requests are handled through a tracked workflow, downstream propagation is documented, and the average response time is within the statutory period. A common failure mode is correcting data in the primary CRM but failing to propagate the update to analytics databases, data warehouses, or third-party processors that received the original data.
exampleEvidence: Rectification request log showing requests received, actioned, and completion dates
Downstream propagation procedure confirming processors are notified of corrections
Self-service correction portal screenshot demonstrating the data subject interface
Example completed rectification request including identity verification and system update evidence
Third-party notification record for a rectification case where data had been shared externally
Data accuracy SLA document specifying maximum time to correct and propagate updates

title: Right to Erasure
description: Data subjects have the right to have their personal data erased without undue delay in specific circumstances, including where data is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, there is no legitimate ground for processing, or an objection is upheld. The right is not absolute and exceptions apply.
guidance: Article 17 lists the grounds for erasure and the exceptions including legal obligation, public interest, and the exercise of legal claims. Where data has been made public or shared with third parties, reasonable steps must be taken to inform them of the erasure request. Organisations must be able to demonstrate that erasure has been carried out, including from backups and archives within a defined timeframe.
implementationGuide: Implement erasure request handling through a dedicated workflow in DataGrail or OneTrust, which should identify all systems holding the individual's data and trigger deletion or anonymisation in each one. Map all personal data stores in advance using BigID or Microsoft Purview so that erasure workflows cover every location, including backups, analytics environments, and third-party processors. Define a backup erasure policy specifying when records deleted from live systems will also be purged from backup media. The 'done' state is when erasure requests trigger automated deletion across all identified systems, backup erasure is scheduled and confirmed, and third-party processors are notified within a defined SLA. A common failure mode is deleting data from the primary database but leaving it accessible in read replicas, cold backups, or analytics snapshots, which means the individual's data persists in practice.
exampleEvidence: Erasure request log with system-by-system deletion confirmation for completed requests
Data map showing all systems queried during the erasure workflow for a given individual
Backup erasure policy defining timescales for purging deleted records from backup media
DataGrail or OneTrust erasure workflow configuration covering all integrated systems
Third-party processor notification template and example notification sent for an erasure case
Erasure procedure document including exceptions register (legal hold, vital interests, etc.)

title: Right to Restriction of Processing
description: Data subjects have the right to request restriction of the processing of their personal data in specified circumstances: where accuracy is contested, processing is unlawful but erasure is opposed, the controller no longer needs the data but the subject requires it for legal claims, or an objection is pending. Restricted data may only be stored and not further processed without consent or specified grounds.
guidance: Article 18 requires the controller to restrict processing while disputes about accuracy or legitimate grounds are resolved. The practical implementation requires a reliable technical mechanism to flag data as restricted so that downstream processing is suspended while the flag is active. Data subjects must be informed before a restriction is lifted.
implementationGuide: Implement a restriction flag in your primary data systems that prevents restricted records from being included in downstream processing, exports, or analytics until the flag is cleared by an authorised reviewer. Log all restriction requests and their resolution in your DSAR management platform such as OneTrust or DataGrail, with automated alerts if a restriction flag has been active beyond a defined review period. Ensure that restricted data remains accessible for storage purposes but is excluded from processing pipelines through access controls or data tagging in Microsoft Purview. The 'done' state is when restriction flags are enforced technically in all key systems and the review log confirms restrictions are resolved within the statutory timeframe. A common failure mode is implementing restriction at the application layer but not in underlying databases or data warehouse extracts, so restricted data continues to flow through analytics pipelines unnoticed.
exampleEvidence: Restriction flag implementation documentation showing which systems support the control
Restriction request log with flag activation dates and resolution records
Technical specification showing how restricted records are excluded from processing pipelines
Sample notification sent to data subject before a restriction was lifted
OneTrust or DataGrail restriction workflow screenshot
Test evidence demonstrating restricted records do not appear in analytical queries or reports

title: Right to Data Portability
description: Where processing is based on consent or contract and is carried out by automated means, data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format, and to have it transmitted to another controller where technically feasible.
guidance: Article 20 applies to data provided by the data subject (not all data held). It does not apply to processing based on legitimate interests or legal obligation. The format requirement (structured, commonly used, machine-readable) means CSV, JSON, or XML are typically acceptable; PDF responses to SARs do not fulfil portability obligations. Where technically feasible, direct transmission to another controller must be facilitated.
implementationGuide: Build or configure a data portability export function in your customer-facing portal that generates a machine-readable export of the data subject's provided data in JSON or CSV format, ensuring the export covers all systems where consent- or contract-based processing occurs. Integrate portability requests into your DSAR management workflow in OneTrust or DataGrail so that portability requests are tracked alongside other rights requests. Document the technical feasibility assessment for direct controller-to-controller transmission for each processing activity. The 'done' state is when data subjects can request and receive a machine-readable export of their data within the statutory period, covering all in-scope systems. A common failure mode is providing a PDF data summary in response to a portability request rather than a structured machine-readable format, which does not satisfy the Article 20 obligation.
exampleEvidence: Data portability export sample showing structured JSON or CSV output for a test subject
Portability request log showing requests fulfilled within the statutory period
Customer portal screenshot demonstrating the portability request and download function
Technical feasibility assessment for direct controller-to-controller transmission
OneTrust or DataGrail portability workflow configuration
Portability procedure document defining in-scope systems, format, and timescales

title: Right to Object
description: Data subjects have the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests or public task, and to object to direct marketing at any time. Objections to direct marketing must be honoured immediately and without exception; objections to other processing must be considered and the processing stopped unless compelling legitimate grounds are demonstrated.
guidance: Article 21 distinguishes between the unconditional right to object to direct marketing and the conditional right to object to other processing. Direct marketing objections must be actioned immediately; no balancing test applies. For other processing, the controller must stop unless it can demonstrate compelling legitimate interests that override the individual's interests, rights, and freedoms, or for the establishment, exercise, or defence of legal claims.
implementationGuide: Implement an objection mechanism in your consent and preference centre (Didomi or OneTrust) that captures marketing opt-outs and routes them to your suppression list within a defined SLA (recommended: same day for marketing). For legitimate interests objections, establish a triage process that differentiates marketing from non-marketing cases and routes non-marketing objections to the DPO for balancing assessment. Maintain a suppression list that is checked before every direct marketing send. The 'done' state is when all direct marketing objections are applied to suppression lists within 24 hours and non-marketing objections are reviewed and resolved within the statutory period. A common failure mode is maintaining suppression lists in individual marketing platforms rather than centrally, resulting in opted-out individuals being re-contacted when a new marketing channel is launched.
exampleEvidence: Objection/opt-out request log showing direct marketing suppression applied within SLA
Central suppression list evidence and integration confirmation across all marketing platforms
DPO balancing assessment record for a non-marketing legitimate interests objection
Preference centre screenshot demonstrating objection mechanism for data subjects
Pre-send suppression check process document and evidence of checks performed
Objection handling policy distinguishing direct marketing from other processing objections

title: Rights Related to Automated Decision-Making
description: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions that produce legal or similarly significant effects. Where such processing occurs, specific safeguards must be in place, including the right to obtain human intervention, to express their point of view, and to contest the decision.
guidance: Article 22 applies where decisions are based solely on automated processing (including profiling) and produce legal or similarly significant effects. Exceptions exist for contractual necessity, legal authorisation, or explicit consent, but safeguards remain required in all cases. The organisation must be able to explain automated decisions meaningfully and provide a route to human review.
implementationGuide: Inventory all automated decision-making systems — including credit scoring, insurance underwriting, recruitment screening, and content moderation — using BigID or Microsoft Purview to identify where personal data flows into algorithmic decision processes. For each system, document whether Article 22 applies, the exception relied upon, and the safeguards in place (human review route, explanation mechanism, contest process). Conduct periodic model fairness and bias assessments for high-risk ADM systems. The 'done' state is when all Article 22 processing activities are identified, safeguards are documented and operational, and data subjects can obtain human review within a defined SLA. A common failure mode is deploying ML models that produce decisions with significant effects without triggering the Article 22 assessment process, particularly where models are developed by engineering teams outside the privacy review workflow.
exampleEvidence: ADM inventory listing all automated decision systems and whether Article 22 applies
Article 22 assessment for each in-scope system with exception and safeguards documented
Human review request procedure and evidence of requests handled within SLA
Model explainability documentation enabling meaningful explanation of automated decisions
Bias and fairness assessment report for a high-risk ADM system
Privacy notice section explaining ADM and data subject rights in plain language

title: Records of Processing Activities
description: Controllers and processors meeting the thresholds in Article 30 must maintain a Record of Processing Activities containing specified information about each processing activity. The ROPA must be kept in writing (including electronic form) and made available to the ICO on request.
guidance: Article 30 requires controllers to record: name and contact details, purposes, categories of data subjects and data, recipients, international transfers, retention timescales, and security measures. Processors maintain a separate record. The ROPA is the foundation of accountability and underpins most other GDPR obligations. It should be treated as a living document, not a one-time exercise.
implementationGuide: Implement a centralised ROPA in a purpose-built privacy management platform such as OneTrust, TrustArc, or Informatica Axon Data Governance to ensure all required Article 30 fields are captured and the record remains current. Assign a ROPA owner within each business unit responsible for submitting new or changed processing activities through a formal intake process. Schedule quarterly reviews to validate that the ROPA reflects current processing, prompted by the privacy team. The 'done' state is when a complete, versioned ROPA is maintained in a single system, all required Article 30 fields are populated, and there is a documented process for updating it when processing changes. A common failure mode is building a comprehensive ROPA at programme launch and then failing to maintain it, so within 12 months the record becomes stale and inaccurate as processing evolves.
exampleEvidence: ROPA export or screenshot showing all Article 30 mandatory fields populated
Business unit ROPA owner register confirming accountability for each section
Quarterly ROPA review minutes or sign-off record
New processing intake form used to add entries to the ROPA
ROPA version history or audit log demonstrating the record is actively maintained
Evidence that ROPA has been made available to ICO or auditor on request

title: Data Protection by Design and Default
description: Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures that are designed to implement data protection principles effectively and integrate necessary safeguards into processing. By default, only personal data necessary for each specific purpose must be processed.
guidance: Article 25 requires privacy to be considered and built in at the design stage of systems and processes, not retrofitted. Data minimisation, pseudonymisation, purpose limitation, and access controls should be the default settings. DPbD assessments should be integrated into the software development lifecycle, product design process, and procurement of new systems.
implementationGuide: Integrate a Privacy by Design checklist into your SDLC gating process — no new product or significant system change should pass through design review without a completed PbD checklist reviewed by the privacy team. Use Microsoft Purview or OneTrust to track PbD assessments linked to project records and ensure findings are formally closed before go-live. For external procurement, include DPbD requirements in RFP questionnaires and vendor assessments. The 'done' state is when PbD checklists are completed for all active development projects, the privacy team is integrated into design review gates, and the SDLC policy formally requires PbD. A common failure mode is treating PbD as a retrospective documentation exercise — completing the checklist after build is complete rather than using it to influence design decisions.
exampleEvidence: Privacy by Design checklist template used in the SDLC review process
Completed PbD checklist for a recent product or system development project
SDLC policy excerpt confirming privacy review is a mandatory gate before go-live
Evidence of PbD assessment influencing a design decision (e.g., field removal, pseudonymisation)
Vendor RFP questionnaire section covering data protection by design requirements
Privacy team sign-off record for new processing activities reviewed prior to launch

title: Data Protection Officer Appointment and Role
description: Organisations that are public authorities, that carry out large-scale systematic monitoring of individuals, or that process special category data on a large scale are required to appoint a Data Protection Officer. The DPO must have expert knowledge of data protection law and practices and be provided with necessary resources.
guidance: Articles 37 to 39 govern DPO appointment, position, and tasks. The DPO must be independent, must not receive instructions on the exercise of their tasks, must not be dismissed or penalised for performing their role, and must report to the highest management level. Contact details must be published and communicated to the ICO. Even where appointment is not mandatory, many organisations appoint a DPO voluntarily.
implementationGuide: Register the DPO's contact details with the ICO using the ICO's online notification service and publish DPO contact information in the organisation's privacy notice and on the website. Ensure the DPO's role description and reporting line are clearly defined in their contract, with explicit protections for independence, access to data processing operations, and resources (budget, staff, tooling). Provide the DPO with access to OneTrust or equivalent to oversee the privacy programme, conduct audits, and track DSR fulfilment. The 'done' state is when the DPO is formally appointed, registered with the ICO, has published contact details, and has a documented reporting line to the board or senior management. A common failure mode is appointing someone with a conflicting role (e.g., Head of IT or Chief Marketing Officer) as DPO, creating an independence conflict that the ICO may consider invalid.
exampleEvidence: DPO appointment letter or contract section confirming role, independence, and reporting line
ICO DPO registration confirmation
Published DPO contact details in privacy notice and/or on organisation website
DPO resource allocation record (budget, tools, staff) for the current financial year
Board or executive meeting minutes showing DPO has direct access to senior management
DPO task log demonstrating active performance of Article 39 responsibilities

title: Staff Training and Awareness
description: All staff who handle personal data as part of their role must receive appropriate data protection training. Training must be role-appropriate, regularly refreshed, and records must be maintained to demonstrate completion. Senior management and specialist roles require more detailed training.
guidance: While not explicitly mandated by a single article, staff training is required to fulfil the accountability principle (Article 5(2)) and is expected by the ICO as a fundamental organisational measure under Article 32. The ICO has cited inadequate staff training as an aggravating factor in enforcement decisions. Training should cover UK GDPR principles, individual rights, breach reporting obligations, and role-specific risks.
implementationGuide: Deploy mandatory annual data protection training through your LMS (e.g., SANS Security Awareness, KnowBe4, or a bespoke eLearning module) with automated completion tracking and escalation to line managers for non-completions after defined reminder cycles. Supplement general training with role-specific modules for high-risk functions such as HR, marketing, IT, and customer services. Brief new starters on data protection during onboarding before they handle personal data. The 'done' state is when annual training completion rates exceed 95% across all staff, completion records are held centrally, and role-specific training is deployed for high-risk roles. A common failure mode is counting training as complete when an employee clicked through a presentation without assessment, providing no assurance that understanding was achieved.
exampleEvidence: LMS training completion report showing completion rates by department and role
Data protection training module content including assessment questions
New starter onboarding checklist confirming data protection briefing before data access
Role-specific training materials for high-risk functions (HR, marketing, IT)
Non-completion escalation record and follow-up evidence
Annual training review record confirming content is updated for regulatory changes

title: Data Protection Policies and Procedures
description: The organisation must maintain a comprehensive suite of data protection policies and operational procedures that give effect to UK GDPR obligations. Policies must be approved at senior level, communicated to staff, and reviewed at defined intervals or when the regulatory or operational landscape changes.
guidance: A policy framework is a core accountability demonstration. Minimum policies typically include: Data Protection Policy, Retention and Disposal Policy, Data Breach Response Policy, DSAR Handling Procedure, and a Privacy Notice Policy. Policies must be operationally effective — accessible to staff, version controlled, and linked to training.
implementationGuide: Maintain a policy library in a document management system (Microsoft SharePoint with Microsoft Purview Compliance Manager, or OneTrust Policy Management) with version control, mandatory review dates, and approval workflows. Assign a policy owner for each document who is accountable for annual reviews and updates triggered by regulatory changes, ICO guidance updates, or operational incidents. Communicate policy updates to all relevant staff through the LMS or intranet. The 'done' state is when a full policy suite covering all key GDPR obligations is in place, every policy has a current approval date within the past two years, and staff can access policies from a central, searchable location. A common failure mode is maintaining policies in a shared drive with no version control or review dates, so it is impossible to demonstrate which version was current at any given point — a significant problem during incident investigation.
exampleEvidence: Data protection policy suite list with version numbers, approval dates, and owners
Senior leadership sign-off for the core Data Protection Policy
Policy review schedule showing next review dates for each policy document
Staff communication record showing policy updates were distributed to relevant personnel
Microsoft SharePoint or OneTrust policy library screenshot showing version-controlled documents
Policy gap analysis confirming all key GDPR obligations are covered by at least one policy

title: Accountability Principle — Demonstrating Compliance
description: The controller is responsible for, and must be able to demonstrate compliance with, all data protection principles. Accountability requires maintaining documentary evidence of compliance, conducting periodic reviews, and ensuring appropriate governance structures are in place.
guidance: Article 5(2) shifts the burden of proof to the controller: it is not sufficient to comply — the organisation must be able to prove it. This requires a well-maintained evidence base including ROPAs, DPIAs, LIAs, training records, policy documents, audit reports, and incident logs. The ICO expects to see a functioning privacy programme with regular reporting to senior management.
implementationGuide: Establish a privacy governance framework with defined roles, a privacy steering committee or working group, and regular reporting to the board through a privacy dashboard in OneTrust or a bespoke reporting template. Conduct an annual internal privacy audit against UK GDPR obligations and document findings, recommendations, and remediation actions with target completion dates. Maintain an evidence library in Microsoft SharePoint or OneTrust that aggregates key compliance artefacts and is reviewed as part of the annual audit cycle. The 'done' state is when a board-level privacy report is produced at least annually, an internal audit report has been completed in the past 12 months, and a structured evidence library supports each key GDPR obligation. A common failure mode is operating a privacy programme without any formal reporting to senior leadership, meaning governance issues are not escalated and resources are not allocated to remediation.
exampleEvidence: Board or executive privacy report produced in the past 12 months
Internal privacy audit report with findings and remediation action plan
Privacy governance framework document defining roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths
Evidence library index demonstrating artefacts exist for each major GDPR obligation
Privacy steering committee meeting minutes from the past two quarters
Remediation tracker showing audit findings being actively progressed to closure

title: DPIA Screening and Threshold Assessment
description: A Data Protection Impact Assessment is mandatory when processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals, and the ICO has published a list of processing types that always require a DPIA. A screening or threshold assessment must be conducted for all new or changed processing activities to determine whether a full DPIA is required.
guidance: Article 35(1) requires a DPIA where processing is 'likely to result in a high risk'. Article 35(3) specifies categories always requiring a DPIA: systematic profiling, large-scale special category processing, and systematic public monitoring. The ICO has published additional criteria. Screening must be documented so that the decision to proceed without a full DPIA is itself defensible.
implementationGuide: Embed a DPIA screening questionnaire into the new processing intake workflow in OneTrust or TrustArc, triggering a full DPIA automatically when screening indicates high risk based on the ICO's nine risk criteria. Ensure the screening form captures the nine factors identified by the ICO (new technology, large scale, systematic monitoring, special categories, etc.) and routes borderline cases to the DPO for a judgment call. Maintain a log of all screening decisions — both those that triggered a full DPIA and those that did not — to demonstrate that screening is occurring systematically. The 'done' state is when every new processing activity passes through the screening process before going live, decisions are logged, and the DPIA screening form covers all ICO mandatory criteria. A common failure mode is embedding screening only in the IT change management process and missing business-led processing changes such as new manual filing systems or outsourced services.
exampleEvidence: DPIA screening questionnaire covering all ICO high-risk criteria
Screening decision log showing all new processing activities assessed in the past 12 months
DPO escalation record for borderline screening cases and documented decisions
DPIA screening process documentation showing integration with change management workflow
ICO mandatory DPIA list mapped to organisation's processing activities
Training material for process owners covering when to trigger a DPIA screening

title: Conducting and Documenting DPIAs
description: Where a DPIA is required, it must describe the processing, assess necessity and proportionality, assess the risks to data subjects, and identify the measures to address those risks. The DPIA must be completed before processing begins. The DPO must be consulted and their advice recorded.
guidance: Article 35(7) specifies the minimum content of a DPIA: a systematic description of the processing, an assessment of necessity and proportionality, risk assessment, and risk mitigation measures. The DPO's advice must be sought (Article 35(2)) and the views of data subjects or their representatives should be sought where appropriate. DPIAs must be reviewed when there is a change to the risk.
implementationGuide: Use a DPIA template aligned to the ICO's published guidance and manage DPIA workflows in OneTrust or TrustArc to ensure all mandatory sections are completed and the DPO review step is mandatory before the DPIA can be marked complete. Assign a DPIA lead from the project team who is responsible for drafting the assessment, and schedule a DPO consultation meeting before the DPIA is finalised. Retain completed DPIAs as part of the accountability evidence base. The 'done' state is when every processing activity that passed the screening threshold has a completed, DPO-reviewed DPIA on file before the processing activity goes live. A common failure mode is completing the DPIA after the system or process has already launched, turning it into a retrospective documentation exercise that cannot influence the design or mitigate risks effectively.
exampleEvidence: Completed DPIA document for a high-risk processing activity with all Article 35(7) sections
DPO consultation record within the DPIA process for an in-scope activity
DPIA template aligned to ICO guidance with mandatory section checklist
DPIA register listing all completed DPIAs with risk rating and residual risk status
Evidence that DPIA was completed before processing went live (e.g., project timeline)
DPIA review record following a material change to an existing processing activity

title: Prior Consultation with ICO
description: Where a DPIA indicates that processing would result in high residual risk that cannot be mitigated, the organisation must consult the ICO before commencing the processing. The ICO has eight weeks (extendable to 14 weeks) to respond with written advice.
guidance: Article 36 applies when a DPIA reveals that the residual risk remains high despite measures taken. Consultation must be initiated before processing begins. The organisation must provide the ICO with the DPIA, contact details, purposes and means of processing, measures to protect data, and DPO contact details. Proceeding without consultation where it is required is a violation and can result in enforcement action.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal decision gate in your DPIA process that requires the DPO and senior legal counsel to sign off when a DPIA concludes with high residual risk, triggering the ICO consultation process before any processing activity is authorised. Maintain a prior consultation register documenting all instances where consultation was considered, the outcome of the assessment, and — where consultation was initiated — the ICO's written response. Document the risk mitigation measures applied to reduce risk to an acceptable level for activities where consultation was considered but ultimately not required. The 'done' state is when no high-residual-risk processing has commenced without either documented risk reduction below the threshold or a completed ICO consultation. A common failure mode is DPIAs being signed off without a formal residual risk assessment step, meaning the need for prior consultation is never explicitly evaluated.
exampleEvidence: Prior consultation register documenting all cases where consultation was considered
ICO consultation submission package for a case that required prior consultation
ICO written response for a completed prior consultation case
DPIA decision gate sign-off record including residual risk assessment
DPO and legal counsel escalation log for high-risk DPIA outcomes
Process documentation for the prior consultation decision gate within the DPIA workflow

title: Technical and Organisational Security Measures
description: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, taking into account the state of the art, costs of implementation, and the nature, scope, context, and purposes of processing.
guidance: Article 32 requires a risk-based approach to security. Measures specified include pseudonymisation and encryption, ensuring ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience, the ability to restore data after an incident, and a process for regular testing and evaluation of security effectiveness. Security must be proportionate to the risk posed by the processing, especially for sensitive data.
implementationGuide: Establish and maintain a TOMs register within your ROPA for each processing activity, documenting the specific security controls applied, using Microsoft Purview Compliance Manager or OneTrust to link TOMs to processing records. Implement encryption at rest (AES-256 or equivalent) and in transit (TLS 1.2+) for all personal data stores, managed through your cloud security platform (e.g., Microsoft Azure Security Centre, AWS Security Hub). Conduct annual penetration testing and vulnerability assessments across in-scope systems, with remediation tracked to closure. The 'done' state is when TOMs are documented for every high-risk processing activity, encryption is verified through automated infrastructure scanning, and the last penetration test report shows no unresolved critical or high findings for personal data systems. A common failure mode is implementing strong controls in production environments but neglecting development and test environments that contain copies of live personal data.
exampleEvidence: TOMs register linked to ROPA processing activities with specific controls documented
Encryption configuration evidence for key personal data stores (at rest and in transit)
Annual penetration test report with remediation status of findings
Microsoft Purview Compliance Manager or equivalent security dashboard screenshot
Vulnerability management report showing personal data systems are within SLA for patching
Business continuity and recovery test evidence demonstrating ability to restore personal data

title: Data Breach Detection and Response
description: The organisation must have processes to detect, investigate, and respond to personal data breaches promptly. All suspected breaches must be assessed to determine whether they meet the threshold for mandatory notification to the ICO and whether affected individuals must be notified.
guidance: Articles 33 and 34 define the notification obligations. A personal data breach is any breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data. Not all breaches require notification — only those likely to result in a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. All breaches, including those below the notification threshold, must be documented in the breach register.
implementationGuide: Deploy a SIEM solution (Microsoft Sentinel, Splunk, or IBM QRadar) with detection rules tuned to personal data exfiltration, unauthorised access, and data integrity events, integrated with a formal incident response playbook that includes a GDPR breach assessment step. Establish a 24/7 breach triage process with a defined escalation path to the DPO within two hours of detection to ensure the 72-hour notification window is achievable. Conduct tabletop exercises simulating data breach scenarios to test the response process. The 'done' state is when a SIEM is operational with personal data breach detection rules, the breach response playbook includes a GDPR assessment step, and at least one tabletop exercise has been conducted in the past 12 months. A common failure mode is detecting a breach through the SIEM but failing to route it to the privacy team in time, because the incident response process treats all security incidents identically without a GDPR triage path.
exampleEvidence: Data breach response playbook including GDPR notification assessment decision tree
SIEM detection rule configuration covering personal data breach scenarios
Tabletop exercise report testing breach detection and notification processes
Breach triage escalation matrix showing DPO notification within the required timeframe
Post-incident review report for a real or simulated breach event
Breach detection-to-DPO notification SLA definition and measurement evidence

title: Notifying the ICO within 72 Hours
description: Where a personal data breach is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller must notify the ICO without undue delay and, where feasible, no later than 72 hours after becoming aware of it. Delayed notifications must be accompanied by reasons for the delay.
guidance: Article 33 requires notification to the ICO containing: nature of the breach, categories and approximate numbers of individuals and records involved, name and contact details of the DPO, likely consequences, and measures taken or proposed. Incomplete information can be provided initially with further details supplied as soon as possible. 'Becoming aware' starts the clock — even before the full extent is known.
implementationGuide: Establish a breach notification decision tree within your incident response playbook that triggers ICO notification assessment automatically when a breach is classified as GDPR-relevant, using OneTrust Incident Management or a dedicated breach tracking module to manage the 72-hour clock. Designate a backup notification contact if the DPO is unavailable, and pre-populate an ICO notification template covering all Article 33(3) required fields. Conduct a quarterly review of the breach register to ensure all notifiable breaches were reported within the deadline. The 'done' state is when every notifiable breach in the past 12 months was reported to the ICO within 72 hours of awareness, or a documented explanation for any delay is on file. A common failure mode is the clock starting from the time the DPO is informed rather than the time any employee became aware, leading to notifications that technically exceed the deadline.
exampleEvidence: ICO breach notification submissions from the past 12 months with timestamps
Breach notification template pre-populated with Article 33(3) required fields
72-hour clock tracking record from awareness to ICO notification for each notifiable breach
Designation of backup notification contact in the incident response playbook
Quarterly breach register review confirming notification timelines were met
OneTrust Incident Management or equivalent breach tracking workflow screenshot

title: Notifying Affected Individuals
description: Where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller must communicate the breach to affected individuals without undue delay. The communication must describe the nature of the breach, the likely consequences, and the measures taken.
guidance: Article 34 applies a higher threshold than ICO notification — only high-risk breaches trigger individual notification. The communication must be in clear and plain language and must include: nature of the breach, DPO contact details, likely consequences, and measures to address the breach and mitigate adverse effects. Exceptions apply where the data was encrypted, subsequent measures eliminated the high risk, or notification would involve disproportionate effort (in which case public communication is required).
implementationGuide: Define a tiered breach communication framework that pre-scripted individual notification templates for common breach scenarios (credential compromise, accidental disclosure, device loss), stored and manageable through OneTrust or your incident management platform. Establish a contact list management process that can rapidly identify affected individuals and their communication preferences at the time of a breach. For breaches involving large populations, engage your communications team to manage public notification where disproportionate effort applies. The 'done' state is when every high-risk breach in the past 12 months resulted in individual notification within a defined timeframe and in the required format, or a documented exception applies. A common failure mode is assessing a breach as 'high risk' for ICO notification purposes but then failing to re-evaluate whether individual notification is also required, because the two thresholds are sometimes confused.
exampleEvidence: Individual breach notification templates for common breach scenarios
Evidence of individual notifications sent for a high-risk breach (anonymised example)
Breach risk assessment documentation distinguishing ICO notification and individual notification thresholds
Contact identification process for rapidly locating affected individuals in a breach
Public notification evidence where disproportionate effort exception was applied
Post-breach individual notification review confirming Article 34 content requirements were met

title: Data Breach Register
description: Controllers must document all personal data breaches, regardless of whether notification to the ICO or individuals is required. The breach register must include the facts of the breach, its effects, and the remedial action taken. It must be available to the ICO on request.
guidance: Article 33(5) requires a comprehensive internal breach register. This is separate from ICO notification — all breaches, including those below the notification threshold, must be logged. The register serves as evidence that the organisation is aware of all breaches and has made deliberate decisions about notification thresholds. It also enables trend analysis and systemic improvement.
implementationGuide: Maintain a breach register in OneTrust Incident Management or a dedicated spreadsheet template that captures: breach date, discovery date, type of breach, data categories affected, estimated number of records and individuals, ICO notification decision and rationale, individual notification decision and rationale, and remedial actions. Review the breach register quarterly with the DPO to identify patterns and systemic weaknesses. Ensure all staff know to report suspected breaches to the privacy team or DPO immediately through a clearly communicated reporting channel. The 'done' state is when a complete breach register is maintained, reviewed quarterly, and every breach — including near-misses — is logged with a notification decision documented. A common failure mode is only logging breaches that were reported to the ICO, leaving a gap in the register for the many lower-risk incidents that did not meet the notification threshold but that Article 33(5) still requires to be documented.
exampleEvidence: Breach register extract showing a range of incidents including sub-threshold events
Breach register template with all Article 33(5) required fields
Quarterly breach register review minutes with DPO sign-off
Staff breach reporting channel documentation (e.g., reporting email, intranet form)
ICO notification decision log for each breach entry (notify / not notify + rationale)
Trend analysis report identifying systemic breach causes and recommended improvements

title: Identifying International Data Transfers
description: The organisation must identify and document all transfers of personal data to countries outside the UK and assess whether each transfer has an appropriate legal basis under Chapter V UK GDPR or the UK's equivalent international transfer framework following the UK GDPR's post-Brexit divergence.
guidance: Chapter V UK GDPR (as retained and amended) restricts transfers of personal data to third countries unless an adequacy decision is in place, appropriate safeguards are used, or a derogation under Article 49 applies. Identification is the prerequisite to compliance — organisations often underestimate the number of international transfers they make, particularly through cloud services, analytics platforms, and third-party integrations.
implementationGuide: Conduct a transfer mapping exercise using BigID or Microsoft Purview to identify data flows that cross UK borders, including processor-to-sub-processor flows that may not be immediately visible to the controller. Document all identified transfers in the ROPA's transfer column, noting the destination country, transfer mechanism, and whether an adequacy decision or other safeguard applies. Repeat the exercise annually and when significant new systems or processors are onboarded. The 'done' state is when all international transfers are documented in the ROPA, each has an identified transfer mechanism, and the transfer mapping has been reviewed within the past 12 months. A common failure mode is failing to capture transfers made by processors and sub-processors on the controller's behalf, particularly cloud infrastructure providers routing data through non-UK data centres.
exampleEvidence: International transfer map showing all identified cross-border data flows and destinations
ROPA extract with transfer destination and mechanism columns populated
BigID or Microsoft Purview data flow scan results identifying international transfers
Annual transfer mapping review record with DPO sign-off
Processor questionnaire used to identify sub-processor international transfers
Data flow diagram illustrating personal data flows involving third countries

title: Adequacy Decisions
description: Where the UK Government has issued an adequacy regulation for a third country or international organisation, personal data may be transferred to that destination without additional safeguards. The organisation must verify the adequacy status of destination countries and monitor for changes.
guidance: Post-Brexit, the UK has its own adequacy framework. The UK Government has issued adequacy regulations for a number of countries (notably EEA/EU, which has reciprocal adequacy for the UK). Adequacy status can be suspended or revoked, as demonstrated by the Court of Justice of the EU's Schrems decisions. Organisations must monitor adequacy decisions actively and have contingency transfer mechanisms in place.
implementationGuide: Maintain an adequacy country register within your transfer map in OneTrust or TrustArc, populated with current UK adequacy decision status sourced from the ICO's published list, and configure automated alerts when a destination country's adequacy status changes. For each adequacy-reliant transfer, document the specific UK adequacy regulation relied upon and the date last verified. Establish a contingency process to implement SCCs or another safeguard within a defined SLA if an adequacy decision is withdrawn. The 'done' state is when all adequacy-reliant transfers are listed in the transfer map with verified decision status, a monitoring mechanism is in place, and contingency safeguards are identified. A common failure mode is relying on EU adequacy decisions rather than verifying the separate UK adequacy position, which diverged following Brexit and covers a different (though largely overlapping) set of countries.
exampleEvidence: Adequacy country register showing UK adequacy status for each transfer destination
ICO adequacy decision list used as the verification source with date of last check
Automated monitoring alert configuration for adequacy decision status changes
Contingency transfer mechanism plan for each adequacy-reliant transfer
ROPA transfer column extract showing adequacy regulation reference for relevant entries
DPO briefing note on the difference between UK and EU adequacy decisions post-Brexit

title: Standard Contractual Clauses
description: Where no adequacy decision exists, the organisation may rely on Standard Contractual Clauses (in the UK context, the International Data Transfer Agreement or the Addendum to EU SCCs) to provide appropriate safeguards for transfers to third countries. SCCs must be in place before the transfer commences.
guidance: The UK has its own transfer mechanism: the International Data Transfer Agreement (IDTA) and the UK Addendum to EU SCCs, both approved by the ICO. These replaced the old EU SCCs for UK-origin transfers. Organisations must ensure they are using the correct UK-approved mechanism and must conduct a Transfer Risk Assessment (TRA) to verify the destination country's laws do not undermine the protections provided by the mechanism.
implementationGuide: Audit all existing transfer agreements to confirm they use the current UK IDTA or UK Addendum rather than outdated EU SCCs, using your contract management system (Ironclad, Juro, or equivalent) with a transfer agreement register in OneTrust. For each new transfer to a non-adequate country, complete a Transfer Risk Assessment before the IDTA is executed, assessing the destination country's surveillance laws, rule of law, and data subject rights. Ensure IDTAs are executed before transfers commence and are stored in the contract management system with linkage to the ROPA entry. The 'done' state is when all non-adequacy transfers are covered by current UK IDTAs or UK Addendum agreements, TRAs have been completed, and the contract register confirms no transfers are occurring under expired EU SCCs. A common failure mode is continuing to rely on EU SCCs for UK-origin transfers without transitioning to the IDTA, which became the required UK mechanism from March 2024.
exampleEvidence: Transfer agreement register confirming all non-adequacy transfers use UK IDTA or UK Addendum
Completed Transfer Risk Assessment (TRA) for a transfer to a high-risk jurisdiction
Executed IDTA with a processor in a non-adequate country
Audit report confirming no UK-origin transfers rely on expired EU SCCs
ICO IDTA template version confirmation in executed agreements
ROPA transfer column extract linking each non-adequacy transfer to its IDTA reference

title: Binding Corporate Rules
description: Multinational organisations may use Binding Corporate Rules as a transfer mechanism for intra-group transfers of personal data to third countries. BCRs must be approved by the ICO (or a lead supervisory authority) and must be legally binding on all group entities.
guidance: Article 47 UK GDPR sets the requirements for BCRs.
