They must be legally binding and enforced by all group members, grant enforceable rights to data subjects, and be approved by the competent supervisory authority. BCR approval is a lengthy process. Organisations using EU-approved BCRs must assess whether UK recognition is in place or whether separate ICO approval is required following Brexit.
implementationGuide: Where BCRs are in place or being sought, maintain a BCR governance framework that includes an internal compliance programme, a designated BCR compliance coordinator, and an annual BCR review submitted to the ICO. Track all intra-group transfers on the BCR in the transfer register within OneTrust, and ensure each group entity has formally adopted the BCR through a binding legal instrument (intra-group agreement or articles of association amendment). For organisations with EU BCRs, assess the post-Brexit UK recognition position with external counsel. The 'done' state is when BCRs are ICO-approved (or recognition is confirmed for existing EU BCRs), all group entities are bound, and an annual compliance review has been submitted. A common failure mode is assuming that EU BCR approval automatically provides a valid UK transfer mechanism without separately confirming the ICO's recognition position.
exampleEvidence: ICO BCR approval letter or confirmation of recognised EU BCR status post-Brexit
Intra-group BCR adoption agreements signed by all group entities
BCR compliance coordinator designation and responsibilities document
Annual BCR compliance review report submitted to the ICO
Transfer register confirming all BCR-covered transfers are documented
External counsel opinion on UK BCR recognition status and any required actions

title: Data Processor Identification and Assessment
description: The organisation must identify all third parties acting as data processors on its behalf and assess whether they provide sufficient guarantees about their technical and organisational security measures and compliance with UK GDPR obligations before engaging them.
guidance: Article 28(1) requires controllers to use only processors providing sufficient guarantees. The assessment must consider security capabilities, sub-processing practices, data breach notification processes, and the ability to support data subject rights. Due diligence should be proportionate to the risk and sensitivity of the processing being outsourced.
implementationGuide: Maintain a processor register as part of your third-party risk management programme, using OneTrust Vendor Risk or similar, categorised by data sensitivity and processing risk level. For high-risk processors, require completion of a data protection due diligence questionnaire covering TOMs, sub-processor lists, breach notification SLAs, and audit rights. For lower-risk processors, rely on their published security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II). Conduct annual re-assessment for high-risk processors. The 'done' state is when every processor handling personal data is registered, a risk-proportionate assessment has been completed, and no high-risk processor has been onboarded without DPO review. A common failure mode is conducting thorough initial due diligence but failing to re-assess processors annually, meaning that changes to their processing activities, security posture, or sub-processor use go undetected.
exampleEvidence: Processor register with all identified processors, data categories handled, and risk tier
Data protection due diligence questionnaire completed by a high-risk processor
ISO 27001 or SOC 2 certificates reviewed for lower-risk processors
Annual re-assessment schedule and evidence of completed re-assessments
DPO review record for new high-risk processor onboarding
Processor risk tiering methodology document

title: Data Processing Agreements with Processors
description: Processing by a processor must be governed by a contract (Data Processing Agreement) that sets out the subject matter, duration, nature and purpose of processing, type of personal data, categories of data subjects, and the obligations and rights of the controller. The DPA must include the mandatory clauses specified in Article 28(3).
guidance: Article 28(3) lists mandatory DPA provisions: process data only on documented instructions; ensure confidentiality; implement Article 32 security measures; respect sub-processor conditions; assist with data subject rights and breach notification; delete or return data at contract end; provide audit information. A DPA that is missing any of these elements does not satisfy Article 28.
implementationGuide: Deploy a standard DPA template that incorporates all Article 28(3) mandatory clauses, maintained by your legal team and version-controlled in your contract management system (Ironclad, Juro, or Microsoft SharePoint). Require every procurement process involving a data processor to include DPA execution as a blocking condition before go-live, enforced through your procurement workflow. Audit the processor register annually to confirm a current, executed DPA is in place for every listed processor. The 'done' state is when every processor in the register has an executed DPA on file, the DPA contains all Article 28(3) elements, and the contract management system confirms no DPAs have expired without renewal. A common failure mode is allowing processors to substitute their own processing terms for the controller's DPA, resulting in agreements that favour the processor and may lack mandatory Article 28 provisions.
exampleEvidence: Standard DPA template with Article 28(3) clauses annotated
DPA contract register confirming executed agreements for all listed processors
Procurement checklist confirming DPA execution is a go-live gate for new processors
Annual DPA audit report confirming no processors are operating without a current DPA
Example executed DPA with a significant processor (redacted as appropriate)
DPA gap analysis report identifying and remediating agreements lacking mandatory clauses

title: Sub-processor Management
description: Processors must not engage sub-processors without the controller's prior specific or general written authorisation. Where general authorisation is given, the processor must inform the controller of any sub-processor changes and the controller must have the opportunity to object. Sub-processors must be bound by equivalent obligations to those imposed on the processor.
guidance: Article 28(2) and (4) govern sub-processing. Most DPAs use general authorisation with a change notification mechanism. The controller must maintain visibility of the sub-processor chain to fulfil its accountability obligations and to ensure that international transfers made by sub-processors have appropriate mechanisms in place.
implementationGuide: Require all DPAs to include a sub-processor notification clause specifying a minimum advance notice period (recommended: 30 days) and confirm the controller's right to object. Maintain a sub-processor list for each significant processor in OneTrust or your contract management system, updated when processors notify changes. When a sub-processor change notification is received, assess the sub-processor's security posture and international transfer implications before deciding whether to object. The 'done' state is when all DPAs contain sub-processor notification clauses, a current sub-processor list exists for each significant processor, and there is a documented process for reviewing and responding to change notifications. A common failure mode is granting general authorisation in the DPA but having no mechanism to track sub-processor notifications, meaning changes happen without the controller's knowledge or any opportunity to object.
exampleEvidence: DPA sub-processor notification clause example confirming advance notice and objection rights
Sub-processor register showing current sub-processors for each significant processor
Sub-processor change notification received from a processor and the controller's response
Assessment process document for reviewing new sub-processor notifications
International transfer assessment triggered by a sub-processor in a non-adequate country
Objection right exercise record or documented waiver decision for a sub-processor change

title: Joint Controller Arrangements
description: Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they are joint controllers and must define their respective responsibilities for compliance with UK GDPR obligations in a transparent arrangement. The essence of the arrangement must be made available to data subjects.
guidance: Article 26 applies when organisations share decision-making about processing purposes and means, rather than one acting as processor for the other. Joint controller arrangements are common in partnerships, data sharing consortia, and online advertising ecosystems. The arrangement must specify who is responsible for providing Article 13/14 information, handling data subject rights, managing breaches, and maintaining the ROPA entries.
implementationGuide: Conduct a joint controller determination exercise for all material data sharing arrangements, using a decision flowchart to assess whether the relationship is controller-to-controller, controller-to-processor, or joint controller, documented in OneTrust or your legal contract management system. For confirmed joint controller arrangements, draft a joint controller agreement (JCA) that allocates each Article 26 responsibility, has been reviewed by both parties' legal counsel, and includes the required data subject-facing summary. The 'done' state is when all joint controller relationships are identified and governed by a signed JCA with responsibilities clearly allocated. A common failure mode is mis-characterising a joint controller relationship as a processor relationship, creating a DPA instead of a JCA, which means the responsibilities and data subject rights mechanisms are incorrectly structured.
exampleEvidence: Joint controller determination exercise results for each material data sharing arrangement
Executed Joint Controller Agreement with responsibility allocation for each Article 26 obligation
Data subject-facing summary of joint controller arrangement published in the privacy notice
Legal review confirming the arrangement is correctly characterised as joint controller
ROPA entries reflecting the joint controller arrangement and shared responsibilities
Contact mechanism enabling data subjects to exercise rights against the designated responsible party

title: Supplier Data Protection Due Diligence
description: The organisation must conduct data protection due diligence on all suppliers that handle personal data before engagement and at defined intervals throughout the relationship. Due diligence must assess the supplier's security posture, compliance programme, incident history, and contractual data protection obligations.
guidance: While not mapped to a single article, supplier due diligence is required by Article 28(1) (processor selection), Article 32 (security), and the accountability principle. The depth of due diligence should be proportionate to the risk: high-risk suppliers (processing sensitive data, large volumes, or critical services) warrant deeper assessment than low-risk suppliers. Due diligence findings must be recorded and inform the risk register.
implementationGuide: Integrate data protection due diligence into your procurement lifecycle using OneTrust Vendor Risk or a comparable platform, triggering a data protection questionnaire for any supplier that will handle personal data. Score suppliers on key risk factors (data sensitivity, volume, security certifications, breach history, sub-processor use, and international transfer exposure) to determine the depth of assessment required. Escalate high-scoring suppliers to the DPO and information security team before contract signature. The 'done' state is when no supplier handling personal data has been onboarded without a data protection due diligence score, high-risk findings have DPO sign-off, and annual reassessments are scheduled for critical suppliers. A common failure mode is conducting rigorous initial due diligence but failing to re-assess suppliers when they undergo significant changes such as mergers, acquisitions, technology platform changes, or new sub-processor relationships.
exampleEvidence: Supplier data protection due diligence questionnaire and scoring methodology
Completed due diligence assessment for a high-risk supplier with DPO sign-off
Procurement workflow evidence showing data protection gate before supplier onboarding
Annual supplier re-assessment schedule and evidence of completed reviews for critical suppliers
Supplier risk register showing data protection scores and remediation actions
Due diligence triggered by a supplier change event (e.g., acquisition or platform migration)
